AUTOMATION_RESEARCH_KIT // KNOWLEDGE_BASE
STATUS: ONLINE // AI_ASSISTANT: ACTIVE

Learning Roadmap: Mastery Path for Automation (Fast-Track & Deep-Track)

Target Audiences: Software developers, hardware engineers, researchers, business professionals
Version: 1.0 | Last Updated: 2026-05-07

Table of Contents

  1. Fast-Track (8-12 Weeks)
  2. Deep-Track (6-12 Months)
  3. Specialized Tracks
  4. Recommended Resources
  5. Milestone Assessments

Fast-Track (8-12 Weeks)

Objective

Acquire breadth across automation fundamentals, implement working projects, understand modern practices. Suitable for career switchers, junior engineers seeking rapid deployment readiness.

Week 1-2: Fundamentals & Python Basics

Learning Objectives:

  • Understand feedback control principles
  • Set up Python development environment
  • Learn NumPy, Matplotlib for data processing & visualization
  • Complete first control simulation

Topics:

  1. Control Theory Intro (2-3 hours)

    • Feedback principle: setpoint, error, feedback
    • Open-loop vs closed-loop control
    • Stability concept
    • Reading: Åström & Murray Ch. 1-2 (40 pages)

  2. Python & Scientific Computing (4-5 hours)

    • Python basics: variables, functions, classes
    • NumPy: arrays, linear algebra
    • Matplotlib: plotting, visualization
    • Jupyter Notebook workflows

  3. Hands-On Lab: Implement temperature controller simulation (setpoint tracking with noise)

    # Pseudocode
temperature = 20  # Current
setpoint = 25     # Desired
for step in range(1000):
    error = setpoint - temperature
    control_signal = Kp * error  # Proportional term
    temperature += 0.1 * control_signal + noise
plot(temperature)

Deliverable: Working Python script demonstrating proportional control with plots

Resources:

  • Coursera: "Python for Data Science" (10 hours)
  • NumPy documentation: numpy.org/doc
  • Åström & Murray Ch. 1-2: feedback-systems.org

Week 3-4: PID Control & Tuning

Learning Objectives:

  • Understand PID controller mathematics
  • Tune controllers experimentally
  • Analyze stability
  • Deploy on simulated systems

Topics:

  1. PID Mathematics (2 hours)

    • Proportional (P): (u = K_p e)
    • Integral (I): (u = K_i \int e dt)
    • Derivative (D): (u = K_d de/dt)
    • Tuning methods: Ziegler-Nichols, loop shaping

  2. Stability Analysis (1.5 hours)

    • Nyquist criterion
    • Bode plots (frequency domain understanding)
    • Root locus basics

  3. Hands-On Labs:

    • Lab 1: Tune PID on simulated mass-spring-damper system
    • Lab 2: Anti-windup design (prevent integral saturation)
    • Lab 3: Robustness analysis (step response, load disturbance)

Deliverable: Tuned PID controller with performance plots (setpoint tracking, disturbance rejection)

Resources:

  • Åström & Murray Ch. 6 (PID Control): 30 pages
  • "PID Controller Tuning" YouTube series: 5 videos × 20 min
  • Open-source: scipy.signal for transfer functions

Week 5-6: ROS2 Fundamentals

Learning Objectives:

  • Install and configure ROS2
  • Understand publish-subscribe architecture
  • Write simple nodes in Python/C++
  • Use Gazebo simulation

Topics:

  1. ROS2 Architecture (2 hours)

    • Node concept (modular components)
    • Topics (pub-sub messaging)
    • Services (request-reply)
    • Parameter server

  2. Practical Setup (1.5 hours)

    • Install ROS2 (Ubuntu 22.04 recommended)
    • Configure workspace
    • Build first package

  3. Hands-On Labs:

    • Lab 1: Create publisher node (sends random numbers)
    • Lab 2: Create subscriber node (receives, averages)
    • Lab 3: TurtleSim tutorial (navigation in virtual environment)
    • Lab 4: Launch multiple nodes; observe communication

  4. Gazebo Simulation (1 hour)

    • Launch simulated robot
    • Send velocity commands
    • Observe sensor data

Deliverable: ROS2 package with 3+ nodes, successful inter-node communication

Resources:

  • ROS2 Official Tutorials: docs.ros.org (12 tutorials)
  • "ROS2 Humble for Beginners" Udemy course (15 hours)
  • Gazebo Documentation: gazebosim.org

Week 7-8: Perception & Machine Learning Basics

Learning Objectives:

  • Understand CNN architecture
  • Deploy pre-trained models
  • Implement object detection
  • Optimize for edge devices

Topics:

  1. Deep Learning Fundamentals (2 hours)

    • Neural networks: neurons, layers, activation functions
    • Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): convolution, pooling, feature extraction
    • Transfer learning: why it works, when to use

  2. Object Detection (1.5 hours)

    • YOLO architecture (single-stage detector)
    • MobileNet (efficient architecture)
    • Inference pipeline: input → model → NMS → output

  3. Hands-On Labs:

    • Lab 1: ImageNet classification with ResNet-50 (PyTorch)
    • Lab 2: YOLO-v5 object detection on webcam feed
    • Lab 3: Accuracy measurement (precision, recall, mAP)
    • Lab 4: Model quantization for edge (INT8 inference)

Deliverable: Working object detector with real-time inference (<100ms latency)

Resources:

  • PyTorch tutorials: pytorch.org/tutorials (8 tutorials)
  • "Fast.ai Practical Deep Learning": fast.ai (30 hours, part 1)
  • YOLOv5 GitHub: ultralytics.com

Week 9-10: Integration Project (Perception → Control)

Learning Objective: Combine perception + control into end-to-end system

Project Options:

Option A: Object Tracking Robot (Simulation)

  • Gazebo simulated mobile robot
  • YOLO detects target object
  • ROS2 node: vision processor (publishes detected location)
  • ROS2 node: motion planner (sends velocity commands to robot)
  • Objective: Robot navigates to target, maintains distance

Option B: Line-Following Robot (Real Hardware)

  • Arduino-based platform (cheap, educational)
  • Webcam or line sensor
  • PID controller tracks line
  • Objective: Follow painted line at constant speed

Option C: Simple Robotic Arm (Simulation)

  • MuJoCo or PyBullet simulation
  • Detect object on table (YOLO)
  • Compute grasp pose (kinematics)
  • Plan trajectory (RRT)
  • Simulate grasp

Specifications:

  • Code: Well-documented, GitHub repo
  • Documentation: README with system description, setup instructions, results
  • Testing: Demonstrate 3+ successful runs; measure performance metrics
  • Debugging log: Known issues and workarounds

Deliverable: Fully functional integrated system with documented results

Evaluation Criteria:

  • Functionality: System operates end-to-end
  • Code quality: Modular, readable, reusable
  • Documentation: Clear instructions for reproduction
  • Testing: Quantitative results (success rate, latency, accuracy)

Week 11-12: Safety, Ethics & Deployment

Learning Objectives:

  • Understand automation safety principles
  • Apply risk assessment
  • Deploy to production-like environment
  • Discuss ethical implications

Topics:

  1. Safety Fundamentals (1.5 hours)

    • FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
    • Risk matrix (severity × likelihood)
    • Safety interlocks, fail-safes
    • IEC 61508 overview

  2. Ethics in Automation (1 hour)

    • Job displacement implications
    • Transparency vs black-box AI
    • Bias in training data
    • Responsible deployment practices

  3. Hands-On Activities:

    • Activity 1: FMEA exercise on your integrated project
    • Activity 2: Identify failure modes (sensor failures, network outage, etc.)
    • Activity 3: Propose mitigations
    • Activity 4: Ethics reflection paper (500 words)

  4. Deployment Preparation (1 hour)

    • Docker containerization
    • CI/CD pipeline overview
    • Version control best practices

Deliverable:

  • FMEA document identifying 10+ failure modes
  • Mitigation strategies for top 3 risks
  • Ethics reflection paper
  • Containerized deployment of week 9-10 project

Resources:

  • "Reliable Robot Perception" Stanford Seminar: 1 hour video
  • IEC 61508 overview documents: free from IEC preview
  • Ethics frameworks: Floridi & Cowley, 2019 (20 pages)

Fast-Track Summary

WeekTopicHoursDeliverable
1-2Python & Control Fundamentals12PID simulator
3-4PID Tuning & Stability10Tuned controller
5-6ROS2 & Gazebo12Multi-node system
7-8Perception & ML12Object detector
9-10Integration Project20End-to-end system
11-12Safety & Ethics8FMEA + ethics reflection
Total74 hoursProduction-ready prototype

Skill Acquisition: Breadth across control, robotics, perception, ML, safety; ready for junior automation engineer role or continued specialization.

Deep-Track (6-12 Months)

Objective

Develop rigorous theoretical foundations and advanced practical skills. Suitable for researchers, senior engineers entering new domains, PhD students.

Month 1-2: Rigorous Control Theory

Learning Objectives:

  • Master state-space methods
  • Understand Lyapunov stability rigorously
  • Design optimal controllers
  • Analyze robustness systematically

Topics:

1. State-Space Methods (2 weeks)

  • State-space representation: (\dot{x} = Ax + Bu), (y = Cx + Du)
  • Controllability and observability (rank conditions)
  • System properties from eigenvalues (stability, time constant)
  • State feedback design: pole placement
  • Observer design: estimate state from limited measurements

Reading: Åström & Murray Ch. 3-5 (100 pages) + Ogata "Control Systems Engineering" Ch. 9-10

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Convert transfer function to state-space (and vice versa)
  • Lab 2: Design state feedback controller via pole placement
  • Lab 3: Design observer; verify observability
  • Lab 4: Combined state feedback + observer (output feedback)

2. Lyapunov Stability (1 week)

  • Lyapunov direct method (construct Lyapunov function)
  • Asymptotic vs global stability
  • Stability of nonlinear systems
  • Robustness margins (gain/phase margins)

Reading: Khalil "Nonlinear Systems" Ch. 2-3 (80 pages)

Hands-On Lab:

  • Lab 1: Prove stability using Lyapunov functions (quadratic form)
  • Lab 2: Robust stability analysis under parameter variations

3. Optimal Control (2 weeks)

  • Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR): minimize (J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u) dt)
  • Riccati equation derivation and solution
  • Model Predictive Control (MPC): receding horizon formulation
  • Robust MPC: constraint tightening for uncertainty

Reading: Åström & Murray Ch. 7 (60 pages) + Boyd "Convex Optimization" Ch. 9

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Design LQR controller; compare performance vs PID
  • Lab 2: Implement MPC using cvxpy or ECOS solver
  • Lab 3: Tune Q, R matrices; analyze trade-offs
  • Lab 4: Robust MPC with disturbance rejection

4. Frequency Domain Methods (1 week)

  • Bode plots, Nyquist plots, gain/phase margins
  • Loop shaping design
  • Compensator design (lead/lag networks)

Reading: Åström & Murray Ch. 4 (60 pages)

Hands-On Lab:

  • Lab 1: Sketch Bode plots by hand; verify with software
  • Lab 2: Design compensator for specified margins
  • Lab 3: Verify Nyquist criterion for stability

Month 1-2 Deliverable: Technical report comparing PID, state feedback, LQR, MPC on benchmark system; derive theoretical insights.

Month 3-4: Advanced Robotics & ROS2 Deep Dive

Learning Objectives:

  • Master ROS2 architecture (DDS, QoS, lifecycle)
  • Implement SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)
  • Develop motion planning algorithms
  • Understand robot kinematics/dynamics

Topics:

1. ROS2 Advanced (2 weeks)

  • DDS (Data Distribution Service): QoS profiles, real-time guarantees
  • Lifecycle nodes: creation → activation → deactivation → cleanup
  • Composition: node runtime vs compile-time
  • Parameter handling and configuration management
  • Testing: pytest + rostest frameworks

Reading: ROS2 documentation "Intermediate Concepts" (200+ pages), ROS2 Design Documentation

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Lifecycle node implementation (robot startup sequence)
  • Lab 2: Composed nodes (multiple processes)
  • Lab 3: QoS experiments (latency vs reliability trade-offs)
  • Lab 4: ROS2 testing framework; write unit tests for nodes

2. Robot Kinematics & Dynamics (2 weeks)

  • Forward kinematics: end-effector position from joint angles
  • Inverse kinematics: joint angles from desired end-effector pose
  • Jacobian matrix: maps joint velocities to end-effector velocities
  • Dynamics: actuator torques from acceleration commands (Lagrangian mechanics)

Reading: Siciliano et al. "Robotics: Modelling, Planning and Control" Part 1 & 2 (300 pages)

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Forward kinematics for 3-DOF arm (DH parameters)
  • Lab 2: Inverse kinematics (analytical + numerical solutions)
  • Lab 3: Jacobian analysis (singularities, workspace)
  • Lab 4: Dynamics simulation (joint-space control → Cartesian forces)

3. SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) (2 weeks)

  • Visual odometry: track camera motion from image sequences
  • Loop closure detection: identify previously visited locations
  • Bundle adjustment: optimize 3D reconstruction
  • Implementation: Cartographer (ROS2), ORB-SLAM2

Reading: Thrun et al. "Probabilistic Robotics" Ch. 10-12 (100 pages) + recent SLAM surveys

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Monocular SLAM on simulation dataset
  • Lab 2: Deploy Cartographer on real robot (TurtleBot3 or similar)
  • Lab 3: Analyze loop closure; verify map consistency
  • Lab 4: Compare visual + LIDAR odometry

4. Motion Planning (2 weeks)

  • Graph-based methods: A*, Dijkstra (grid/graph search)
  • Sampling-based methods: RRT, RRT* (probabilistically complete)
  • Trajectory optimization: quintic polynomials, time-optimal trajectories
  • MoveIt framework: integrate planning with ROS2

Reading: LaValle "Planning Algorithms" Ch. 2-5 (200 pages) + MoveIt tutorials

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Implement RRT from scratch; benchmark vs RRT*
  • Lab 2: MoveIt motion planning for 7-DOF robot arm
  • Lab 3: Obstacle avoidance with RRT+collision checking
  • Lab 4: Trajectory execution on real/simulated robot

Month 3-4 Deliverable: Autonomous navigation demo (SLAM + motion planning; robot maps environment, reaches goal)

Month 5-6: Machine Learning for Control

Learning Objectives:

  • Master deep reinforcement learning algorithms
  • Implement safe RL with constraints
  • Understand imitation learning
  • Apply ML to control problems

Topics:

1. Deep Reinforcement Learning (2 weeks)

  • MDPs (Markov Decision Processes): states, actions, rewards, transitions
  • Value iteration & policy iteration (discrete, tractable)
  • Q-learning (model-free, off-policy)
  • Policy gradient methods (PG, A3C)
  • Actor-Critic methods (A2C, A3C, PPO)

Reading: Sutton & Barto "Reinforcement Learning" Ch. 3-13 (400 pages) + Deep RL survey papers

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Gridworld environment; implement value iteration
  • Lab 2: CartPole with Q-learning (discrete approximation)
  • Lab 3: Implement PPO from scratch (or use stable-baselines3)
  • Lab 4: Continuous control: CartPole + inverted pendulum

Performance Targets:

  • CartPole-v1: 500 reward (max); PPO achieves this in 50K steps
  • MuJoCo benchmark: Compare to published baseline numbers

2. Safe Reinforcement Learning (2 weeks)

  • Constrained MDPs (CMDPs): optimize reward subject to constraint
  • Lagrangian relaxation: dual formulation + gradient-based optimization
  • Control Barrier Functions (CBF): formal safety certificates
  • Shielding: add safety monitor to learned policy
  • Curriculum learning: gradually increase task difficulty

Reading: Garcia & Fernández "Safe RL" survey (2015) + recent papers (2020-2024)

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Implement constrained PPO (using Lagrangian relaxation)
  • Lab 2: Compare constrained vs unconstrained RL (CartPole with "no flip" constraint)
  • Lab 3: Design CBF for simple system; verify safety
  • Lab 4: Curriculum learning: start with easy task, gradually increase difficulty

Key Metric: Maintain 100% safety constraint satisfaction while maximizing reward

3. Imitation Learning & Behavior Cloning (1 week)

  • Behavior cloning: learn policy by imitating expert demonstrations
  • Dataset aggregation (DAgger): address distribution mismatch
  • Inverse reinforcement learning: infer reward function from demonstrations

Reading: Torabi et al. "Behavioral Cloning from Observation" (15 pages)

Hands-On Lab:

  • Lab 1: Collect expert demonstrations (PyGame interface or kinesthetic teaching)
  • Lab 2: Train behavior cloning model
  • Lab 3: Evaluate and identify failure modes
  • Lab 4: Implement DAgger to reduce distribution mismatch

4. Sim2Real Transfer (2 weeks)

  • Domain randomization: train on randomized simulations
  • System identification: estimate real-world parameters
  • Reality gap analysis: quantify transfer degradation
  • Continual adaptation: online learning on real system

Reading: Tobin et al. "Domain Randomization for Sim2Real Transfer" (2017) + recent survey papers

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Train policy in simulation (no randomization); measure transfer failure
  • Lab 2: Train with domain randomization; compare transfer success
  • Lab 3: Quantify reality gap (measure <3% target)
  • Lab 4: Implement online adaptation on real robot

Month 5-6 Deliverable: RL-trained controller for simulated/real system with safety guarantees; publish results.

Month 7-8: Systems Integration & Real Automation

Learning Objectives:

  • Program PLCs and industrial controllers
  • Configure SCADA systems
  • Integrate edge AI hardware
  • Deploy production automation systems

Topics:

1. PLC Programming (1.5 weeks)

  • IEC 61131-3 languages: ladder logic, structured text, function blocks
  • State machine implementation
  • Timing/sequencing logic
  • Interface with sensors/actuators via I/O modules

Hands-On Lab (using OpenPLC simulator or physical PLC):

  • Lab 1: Basic ladder logic (AND, OR, NOT gates)
  • Lab 2: State machine: (Idle → Running → Error → Reset)
  • Lab 3: Scan cycle understanding (deterministic timing)
  • Lab 4: Integrate with industrial simulation (Factory I/O)

2. SCADA & Industrial Protocols (1.5 weeks)

  • SCADA architecture: RTU/PLC ↔ communication network ↔ control center
  • OPC UA: semantic industrial data models
  • Modbus/Profibus: legacy protocols still widely used
  • Cybersecurity: IEC 62443 (functional security levels)

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: OPC UA server implementation
  • Lab 2: OPC UA client (connect to real PLC or simulator)
  • Lab 3: Modbus gateway (translate OPC UA ↔ Modbus)
  • Lab 4: Implement basic IEC 62443 security (authentication, encryption)

3. Edge AI Deployment (1.5 weeks)

  • Deploy RL/CV models on NVIDIA Jetson (or Google Coral)
  • Model optimization: quantization, pruning, TensorRT
  • Real-time scheduling: ensure latency budgets met
  • Power/thermal management

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Convert PyTorch model to ONNX format
  • Lab 2: Optimize for Jetson using TensorRT
  • Lab 3: Measure latency, memory, power on device
  • Lab 4: Deploy end-to-end system (perception + control) on edge

4. Cloud & Orchestration (1.5 weeks)

  • Docker containerization of automation components
  • Kubernetes for scale-out deployment
  • Digital twin platforms (NVIDIA Omniverse, Siemens MindSphere)
  • Edge-cloud hybrid architecture

Hands-On Labs:

  • Lab 1: Docker containerize perception node
  • Lab 2: Multi-container deployment (edge device)
  • Lab 3: Cloud synchronization (edge ↔ cloud)
  • Lab 4: Understand latency/bandwidth trade-offs

Month 7-8 Deliverable: End-to-end industrial automation system (PLC → SCADA → Edge AI ↔ Cloud); document architecture and performance.

Month 9-10: Advanced Topics & Specialization

Choose One Deep Specialization:

Option A: Formal Verification & Safe Autonomy

  • Formal methods (temporal logic, model checking)
  • Neural network verification
  • Hybrid system verification
  • Application to autonomous systems

Reading: Clarke et al. "Model Checking" (400 pages) + NN verification papers

Option B: Multi-Robot Coordination

  • Decentralized control architectures
  • Graph algorithms for coordination
  • Game-theoretic formulations
  • Swarm robotics

Reading: Bullo et al. "Distributed Control of Robotic Networks" (200 pages)

Option C: Advanced Perception

  • 3D object detection (PointNet, PointPillars)
  • Semantic/instance segmentation
  • Optical flow and motion estimation
  • Sensor fusion (cameras + LIDAR + radar)

Reading: Recent survey papers on 3D perception (2022-2024)

Option D: Manufacturing Optimization

  • Digital twins for optimization
  • Predictive maintenance (remaining useful life)
  • Demand forecasting & production scheduling
  • Quality control via computer vision

Reading: Industry 4.0 case studies + optimization textbooks

Month 9-10 Deliverable: Specialized deep-dive report; implement proof-of-concept; position for publication.

Month 11-12: Research & Contribution

Learning Objectives:

  • Identify novel research question
  • Implement rigorous experiments
  • Write academic paper
  • Contribute to open-source

Activities:

1. Literature Review (2 weeks)

  • Identify knowledge gap in automation
  • Read 30+ papers (background + recent)
  • Write literature review (20-30 pages)
  • Identify specific research question

2. Method Development (2 weeks)

  • Propose novel approach/algorithm
  • Theoretical analysis (if applicable)
  • Comparison to prior methods

3. Experimentation & Evaluation (4 weeks)

  • Implement method
  • Run benchmarks against baselines
  • Statistical significance testing
  • Ablation studies

4. Paper Writing & Publication (2 weeks)

  • Write paper (8-12 pages, top-tier venue format)
  • Submit to conference/journal (e.g., ICRA, IROS, CoRL, IJRR)
  • Prepare code/data for reproducibility

5. Open-Source Release (1 week)

  • Clean code, remove proprietary components
  • Write README, tutorials
  • Publish on GitHub under MIT/Apache 2.0
  • Document API

Month 11-12 Deliverable:

  • Published paper (or under review)
  • Open-source code with 100+ GitHub stars (within 1 year post-release)
  • Potential conference talk/presentation

Specialized Tracks

Software Developer → Automation Expert (16 weeks)

WeekFocusRationale
1-4Control fundamentals + PythonSkip advanced math; emphasize practical coding
5-8ROS2 + robotics softwareLeverage existing software skills
9-12ML/RL for controlRecent field; aligns with modern automation
13-16Systems integration + deploymentIndustry skills valued in job market

Recommended Path: Fast-track (8-12 weeks) + Months 3-4 (ROS2 deep) + Month 7-8 (edge deployment)

Hardware Engineer → Automation Expert (16 weeks)

WeekFocusRationale
1-2Control fundamentalsTheory foundation
3-4PID tuning on real hardwareBuild on existing electronics skills
5-6ROS2 (less deep)Learn software abstraction
7-8PLC + industrial controllersNatural progression from microcontrollers
9-12Robotics (kinematics/dynamics)Physical systems understanding
13-16Systems integrationCombine hardware + software knowledge

Recommended Path: Months 1-2 (control theory) + 3-4 (robotics) + 7-8 (PLC) + 9-10 (hardware specialization)

Manager / Business Professional (6 weeks)

WeekFocusContent
1Automation landscapeHistory, market size, adoption rates
2Technology overviewControl, robotics, AI, RPA landscape
3Business cases & ROICost-benefit analysis, implementation timelines
4Industry examplesManufacturing, RPA, autonomous vehicles
5Workforce implicationsJob displacement, reskilling strategies
6Implementation roadmapRisk management, governance, vendor selection

Recommended Reading:

  • McKinsey "The Future of Work" (2024)
  • Gartner Robotics Market Report
  • Industry case studies (Siemens Smart Factory, Amazon Robotics, Waymo)

Deliverable: Implementation roadmap for automation initiative in own organization

Recommended Resources

Textbooks (Foundational)

Control Theory:

  • Åström & Murray (2021) "Feedback Systems": Practical, modern perspective. 600 pages. FREE online: feedback-systems.org
  • Ogata "Control Systems Engineering" (2010): Industry-standard. 900+ pages. $150.
  • Khalil "Nonlinear Systems" (2014): Advanced, rigorous. 700 pages. $130.

Robotics:

  • Siciliano et al. (2009) "Robotics: Modelling, Planning and Control": Comprehensive. 600 pages. $120.
  • Craig "Introduction to Robotics" (2005): Foundational, accessible. 400 pages. $100.
  • LaValle "Planning Algorithms" (2006): Motion planning focus. 800+ pages. FREE online: lavalle.pl

Reinforcement Learning:

  • Sutton & Barto (2018) "Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction": 500+ pages. ~$70. Also available free draft.
  • Goodfellow et al. (2016) "Deep Learning": 800 pages. $80. Also free HTML version.

Online Courses (High-Quality)

Free:

  • MIT OpenCourseWare "Control of Mobile Robots": Taught by Magnus Egerstedt. 12 lectures + assignments.
  • Stanford "Feedback Systems" YouTube lectures: Åström teaching his own book.
  • Fast.ai "Practical Deep Learning": Jeremy Howard. Top-down approach, strong pedagogical design.

Paid (but affordable):

  • Coursera "Control of Mobile Robots" (Egerstedt): $40-50. Includes quizzes + certificate.
  • Udacity "Self-Driving Car Engineer": $200-400. Comprehensive, well-designed.
  • Udemy "ROS2 Humble for Beginners": $15-20 (frequent sales). Hands-on video tutorials.

Research Communities & Conferences

Conferences (Top-tier):

  • ICRA (Int'l Conf. on Robotics & Automation): Robotics general, rigorous peer review
  • IROS (Int'l Conf. on Intelligent Robots and Systems): Autonomous systems, applications
  • RSS (Robotics: Science and Systems): Rigorous, selective. Best papers often shape field
  • NeurIPS (Neural Information Processing Systems): ML focus; increasingly robotics
  • CoRL (Conf. on Robot Learning): RL for robotics, emerging venue

Journals (High-impact):

  • IEEE Transactions on Robotics: Robot kinematics, dynamics, control
  • Int'l Journal of Robotics Research: Rigorous, application-focused
  • Autonomous Robots: General robotics and automation
  • IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control: Control theory and applications

Open Communities:

  • ROS Discourse (discourse.ros.org): 10,000+ members, active support
  • Reddit r/robotics: 100K members; beginner-friendly
  • GitHub: Follow influential researchers (leave stars, contribute issues)
  • ArXiv: Read preprints; sub-communities exist for control, robotics, ML

Hands-On Platforms

Simulation:

  • Gazebo: Free, open-source, ROS-integrated. Used by researchers + industry.
  • PyBullet: Python physics engine; minimal setup, rapid prototyping.
  • MuJoCo: Advanced physics, differentiable simulation. Increasingly popular for RL.
  • Webots: Educational, modern, supports ROS.

Real Hardware (Affordable):

  • TurtleBot3: $300-500. Mobile robot with ROS support. Ideal for learning.
  • Arduino: $25-50. Microcontroller boards. Ideal for embedded systems.
  • NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano: $200-350. Edge AI platform. Learning kit available.
  • Universal Robots UR5e: $35,000+. Collaborative arm. Many universities have demos.

Practice Problems & Datasets

Control Benchmarks:

  • Benchmark environments in Gymnasium (formerly OpenAI Gym): CartPole, Pendulum, MuJoCo tasks
  • Performance comparison: Papers publish baselines; reproduce them as practice

Robotics Datasets:

  • KITTI: Autonomous driving dataset. 80GB+. Benchmark for 3D object detection.
  • nuScenes: Autonomous driving dataset. Larger, more diverse than KITTI.
  • RoboNet: Robot interaction dataset. 15M frames. For learning manipulation.

RPA Datasets:

  • Open datasets limited (proprietary); practice on public websites (with permission)
  • Build your own automation workflow on test sites

Milestone Assessments

Fast-Track Checkpoints

Week 2 Checkpoint:

  • Implement PID controller in simulation
  • Tune controller to reduce steady-state error <5%
  • Plot step response with/without controller
  • Goal: Demonstrate proportional control concept

Week 4 Checkpoint:

  • Tune PID (P, I, D terms individually understood)
  • Analyze system stability (Nyquist/Bode)
  • Compare performance vs baseline PID
  • Goal: Master feedback control

Week 6 Checkpoint:

  • ROS2 package with 3+ nodes
  • Pub-sub communication between nodes verified
  • Gazebo simulation running
  • Goal: ROS2 proficiency for robotics development

Week 8 Checkpoint:

  • Object detection model running in real-time
  • Inference latency <100ms
  • Accuracy >85% on test set
  • Goal: Deploy ML model for perception

Week 10 Checkpoint:

  • Integration project complete and functional
  • Documentation clear (README, diagrams)
  • 3+ successful runs with quantitative metrics
  • Goal: End-to-end system ownership

Week 12 Checkpoint:

  • FMEA document with 10+ failure modes
  • Safety mitigations proposed and evaluated
  • Ethics reflection demonstrates critical thinking
  • Goal: Safety & responsibility awareness

Deep-Track Checkpoints

Month 2 Checkpoint (Control Theory):

  • State-space controller designed and simulated
  • LQR controller outperforms PID on benchmark
  • Stability analysis with Lyapunov functions completed
  • Frequency domain design (Bode/Nyquist) verified

Month 4 Checkpoint (Robotics):

  • SLAM algorithm implemented; map generated
  • Motion planning (RRT/MoveIt) produces collision-free paths
  • Kinematics forward/inverse verified
  • End-to-end: navigation demo (map → plan → execute)

Month 6 Checkpoint (ML for Control):

  • RL agent trained and evaluated
  • Constrained RL maintains safety constraints 100%
  • Sim2real transfer gap <5% (target <3%)
  • Paper/report documenting results

Month 8 Checkpoint (Systems Integration):

  • PLC program runs correctly on real/simulated hardware
  • OPC UA communication established
  • Edge AI model deployed and latency verified
  • End-to-end industrial system operational

Month 12 Checkpoint (Research):

  • Paper submitted to peer-reviewed venue
  • Code released as open-source
  • 2+ citations to published work (if accepted)
  • GitHub repository with 50+ stars (trajectory toward 100+)

Conclusion

Fast-Track (8-12 weeks): Acquire breadth; ready for junior automation engineer role. Focus: working prototype with modern practices.

Deep-Track (6-12 months): Develop research capability; ready for senior engineer / PhD researcher role. Focus: theoretical rigor, novel contributions, publication.

Key to Success:

  1. Hands-on projects: Theory + practice, not lecture-only
  2. Iterate rapidly: Build → test → debug → improve
  3. Share knowledge: Write documentation, contribute to open-source
  4. Connect with community: Conferences, online forums, local meetups
  5. Stay current: Read recent papers, follow industry developments

Last updated: 2026-05-07 | MIT License
